summary:
The Second Opium War (1856–60), fought by Britain and France against Qing China, was the sequel to the [[event:2541|First Opium War]] and further extended the Western powers' rights and privileges in China through a new set of unequal treaties. It is remembered particularly for the sacking and destruction of the [[event:3499|Old Summer Palace]] by British and French forces — an act that the Chinese government has described as cultural desecration and that remains a source of national grievance.

The war began with two separate incidents. The Arrow Affair involved a vessel flying British colors searched by Chinese authorities without British permission — Britain used this as a pretext for demanding further treaty concessions. France joined after a French missionary was executed in Guangxi province. British and French forces quickly captured Canton and Tianjin, forcing the Qing government to sign the Treaties of Tianjin in 1858, which opened additional ports, allowed foreign ambassadors to reside in Beijing (a significant concession), permitted Christian missionaries throughout China, and legalized the opium trade.

When the Qing government balked at ratifying the treaties and resisted the ambassadors' entry to Beijing with force — inflicting a notable defeat on a British-French force at the Taku forts — the war resumed in 1860. An Anglo-French army captured Tianjin and marched on Beijing. Emperor Xianfeng fled. As a reprisal for the torture and death of Western envoys who had been captured during negotiations, Lord Elgin ordered the destruction of the Old Summer Palace, one of the architectural wonders of the Qing Empire.

The resulting Convention of Peking added further concessions: the cession of Kowloon to Britain, additional ports opened, the right of Chinese to emigrate as contract laborers, and expanded Christian missionary rights. The cumulative effect of the two Opium Wars and subsequent treaties established the unequal treaty system that defined China's foreign relations until the twentieth century.
